title: Sort array of 0s and 1s
description: Sort an array containing only 0s and 1s efficiently

examples.explanation: Initial: [0, 1, 0]; Pass 1: Compare 0-1: 0>1? No → [0, 1, 0]; Compare 1-2: 1>0? Yes, swap → [0, 0, 1]; Pass 2: Compare 0-1: 0>0? No → [0, 0, 1]; Final: [0, 0, 1]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²) with Bubble Sort
approaches.summary: Use a simple sorting algorithm like bubble sort to repeatedly swap adjacent elements until the array is sorted. This is inefficient but straightforward for educational purposes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Array of 0s and 1s Using Bubble Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of bubble sort to sort an array containing only 0s and 1s.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the problem using the bubble sort algorithm. The intuition is that bubble sort repeatedly passes through the array, comparing adjacent elements and swapping them if they're in the wrong order. For an array of 0s and 1s, this means we want all 0s to come before all 1s, so we swap whenever we find a 1 followed by a 0. The algorithm works by making multiple passes through the array, with each pass 'bubbling' the largest unsorted element (in this case, 1s) to its correct position at the end. After n-1 passes, the array will be completely sorted. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Get the length n of the array. 2. For i from 0 to n-2 (outer loop for passes): a. For j from 0 to n-i-2 (inner loop for comparisons): i. If arr[j] > arr[j+1] (which means 1 followed by 0), swap them. 3. Return the sorted array. Time complexity is O(n²) because we have nested loops each potentially iterating n times. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra space for temporary variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete bubble sort algorithm that uses the key swapping subroutine. Common mistakes include: 1. Off-by-one errors in loop bounds (should be n-1 for outer loop, n-i-1 for inner loop). 2. Not optimizing by stopping early if no swaps occur in a pass (though not required for correctness). 3. Confusing the comparison condition (should be > not < since we want 0s before 1s). Real code pattern: def sort_binary_array(arr): n = len(arr); for i in range(n-1): for j in range(n-i-1): if arr[j] > arr[j+1]: arr[j], arr[j+1] = arr[j+1], arr[j]; return arr

examples.trace: Initial: [0, 1, 0]; Pass 1: Compare 0-1: 0>1? No → [0, 1, 0]; Compare 1-2: 1>0? Yes, swap → [0, 0, 1]; Pass 2: Compare 0-1: 0>0? No → [0, 0, 1]; Final: [0, 0, 1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bubble Sort Pass with Adjacent Swapping
ladders.desc: Implement a single pass of bubble sort that compares and swaps adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the key subroutine of bubble sort: performing one complete pass through the array to compare adjacent elements and swap them if they're in the wrong order. The intuition is that by making one pass from left to right, we can ensure that at least the largest element (in this context, the rightmost 1 that should be at the end) moves to its correct position. For binary arrays, we specifically swap when we encounter a 1 followed by a 0, since we want all 0s before all 1s. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Take an array arr of length n. 2. For i from 0 to n-2: a. Compare arr[i] and arr[i+1]. b. If arr[i] > arr[i+1] (which means 1 followed by 0), swap them. 3. Return the modified array. Time complexity is O(n) for a single pass. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 as it's the inner loop of the complete bubble sort algorithm. In the full solution, we repeat this pass n-1 times. This connects to L2 as it implements the core swapping logic. Common mistakes include: 1. Going out of bounds by iterating to n-1 instead of n-2 (since we compare i and i+1). 2. Forgetting that swaps modify the array in-place. 3. Incorrect comparison operator (should be > for moving 1s right, not <). Real code pattern: def bubble_pass(arr): n = len(arr); for i in range(n-1): if arr[i] > arr[i+1]: arr[i], arr[i+1] = arr[i+1], arr[i]; return arr

examples.trace: Initial: [1, 0, 1, 0]; Compare index 0-1: 1>0? Yes, swap → [0, 1, 1, 0]; Compare 1-2: 1>1? No → [0, 1, 1, 0]; Compare 2-3: 1>0? Yes, swap → [0, 1, 0, 1]; Final after one pass: [0, 1, 0, 1]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare and Swap Adjacent Elements Conditionally
ladders.desc: Compare two adjacent elements and swap them only if the left is greater than the right.
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic of bubble sort: comparing two adjacent elements and deciding whether to swap them based on their order. The intuition is that in a sorted binary array, all 0s should come before all 1s. Therefore, if we ever find a 1 (value 1) immediately followed by a 0 (value 0), these elements are in the wrong order and need to be swapped. The comparison arr[i] > arr[i+1] evaluates to true exactly when arr[i] is 1 and arr[i+1] is 0, since 1 > 0. When this condition is met, we perform a swap to correct the inversion. The step-by-step algorithm for a single comparison-swap operation: 1. Take two adjacent elements at positions i and i+1. 2. Check if arr[i] > arr[i+1]. 3. If true, swap their values: store arr[i] in temp, assign arr[i+1] to arr[i], then assign temp to arr[i+1]. 4. If false, do nothing. Time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison-swap. Space complexity is O(1) for the temporary variable. This connects to L1 as it's the fundamental operation performed in each iteration of the bubble pass. This connects to L3 as it relies on understanding element comparison and swapping. Common mistakes include: 1. Using the wrong comparison operator (e.g., < instead of > would sort in descending order). 2. Incorrect swap implementation that loses one value. 3. Not considering that this only works for adjacent elements. Real code pattern: if arr[i] > arr[i+1]: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[i+1]; arr[i+1] = temp

examples.trace: Initial: arr[0]=1, arr[1]=0; Compare: 1 > 0? Yes; Swap: temp=1; arr[0]=0; arr[1]=1; Final: arr[0]=0, arr[1]=1
Initial: arr[0]=0, arr[1]=1; Compare: 0 > 1? No; No swap; Final: arr[0]=0, arr[1]=1

ladders.title: Building Block — Swap Two Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Swap the values at two given positions in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is an intermediate concept: swapping two elements in an array. The intuition is that to exchange values between two positions, we need a temporary variable to hold one value while we overwrite it with the other value, then assign the held value to the other position. Without a temporary variable, we would lose one of the values during the swap. For example, if we try to swap a and b by doing a = b then b = a, both end up with b's original value. The correct three-step process preserves both values. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Given an array arr and two indices i and j. 2. Store arr[i] in a temporary variable temp. 3. Assign arr[j] to arr[i]. 4. Assign temp to arr[j]. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the temporary variable. This connects to L2 as swapping is the action taken when the comparison condition is true. This connects to L4 as it uses variable assignment operations. Common mistakes include: 1. Forgetting to use a temporary variable, leading to lost data. 2. Swapping the same element with itself unnecessarily. 3. Assuming swap works for any data type without considering mutability. Real code pattern: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp

examples.trace: Initial: arr=[5,10]; temp = arr[0] → temp=5; arr[0] = arr[1] → arr[0]=10; arr[1] = temp → arr[1]=5; Final: [10,5]
Initial: arr=[1,2,3]; temp = arr[0] → temp=1; arr[0] = arr[2] → arr[0]=3; arr[2] = temp → arr[2]=1; Final: [3,2,1]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Determine if one number is greater than another.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation: comparing two numbers to determine their relative order. The intuition is that in programming, we often need to make decisions based on whether one value is larger, smaller, or equal to another. For sorting, we specifically care about the greater-than (>) comparison to identify when elements are out of order. In the context of binary arrays containing only 0s and 1s, the comparison is simple: 1 > 0 is true, 0 > 1 is false, and any comparison with equal numbers (0 > 0 or 1 > 1) is false. This boolean result (true or false) then controls whether we perform a swap in the sorting algorithm. The step-by-step process: 1. Take two numbers a and b. 2. Evaluate the expression a > b. 3. The result is true if a is numerically greater than b, false otherwise. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as comparisons are used to decide whether to swap elements. This connects to L5 as it builds on understanding numerical values. Common mistakes include: 1. Confusing comparison operators (e.g., using >= instead of >, which would treat equal elements as out of order). 2. Not understanding that comparison returns a boolean value. 3. Overcomplicating with if-else statements when a direct boolean expression suffices. Real code pattern: result = a > b

examples.trace: Compare: 5 > 3? Since 5 is greater than 3, the expression evaluates to true.
Compare: 2 > 7? Since 2 is not greater than 7, the expression evaluates to false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Binary Values (0 and 1)
ladders.desc: Recognize and work with the binary values 0 and 1 as numerical data.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding that 0 and 1 are numerical values that can be stored in variables and compared. The intuition is that in computer science, we often use binary values to represent states, and in this problem, the array contains only these two values. 0 and 1 are integers, with 0 being less than 1. This fundamental understanding is crucial because the sorting algorithm relies on comparing these values to determine order. For example, knowing that 1 > 0 is true allows us to identify when a 1 appears before a 0, which is the wrong order for a sorted array where all 0s should come first. The step-by-step learning: 1. Recognize that 0 and 1 are numbers like any other integers. 2. Understand that 0 represents a smaller value than 1. 3. Practice storing 0 and 1 in variables. 4. Practice retrieving these values from array indices. Time and space analysis are not applicable here as this is a conceptual foundation. This connects to L4 as it provides the basic values that will be compared. Common mistakes include: 1. Treating 0 and 1 as strings or characters instead of numbers. 2. Confusing binary values with boolean true/false (though they can be related). 3. Not realizing that comparisons work numerically. Real code pattern: a = 0; b = 1; arr = [0, 1, 0]

examples.input: Store 0 in variable x and 1 in variable y
examples.trace: Assign 0 to x: x = 0; Assign 1 to y: y = 1; Result: x holds 0, y holds 1.
Initialize array: arr = []; Set arr[0] = 1; Set arr[1] = 0; Set arr[2] = 1; Final array: [1, 0, 1].

testCases.input: Store 0 in variable x and 1 in variable y

approaches.name: Two-Pass Counting O(n)
approaches.summary: First, count the number of 0s and 1s in the array. Then, overwrite the array by placing all 0s first followed by all 1s based on the counts. This is efficient and easy to implement.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Array of 0s and 1s Using Two-Pass Counting
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of sorting an array containing only 0s and 1s using the two-pass counting approach.
ladders.explanation: The two-pass counting approach solves the sorting problem efficiently by leveraging the fact that we only have two distinct values (0 and 1). The intuition is simple: if we know exactly how many 0s are in the array, we can fill the beginning of the array with that many 0s, and the rest with 1s. This works because the sorted order requires all 0s to come before all 1s. The algorithm proceeds in two clear phases: first, we traverse the array once to count the number of 0s (and implicitly, 1s by subtracting from total length). Second, we traverse the array again, overwriting positions from the start with 0s until we've placed all counted 0s, then fill the remaining positions with 1s. This approach is O(n) time complexity because we make two passes through the array, each of linear time. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a couple of integer variables for counting, regardless of input size. Connection to L1: This full solution directly uses the counting subroutine from L1 to determine how many 0s are present. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors when filling the array (e.g., miscounting indices), forgetting to handle edge cases like empty arrays, or incorrectly calculating the number of 1s. To avoid these, carefully track loop boundaries and validate counts. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def sort_zeros_ones(arr): count_zeros = 0; for num in arr: if num == 0: count_zeros += 1; for i in range(len(arr)): if i < count_zeros: arr[i] = 0; else: arr[i] = 1; return arr.

examples.trace: Step 1: Count zeros. Traverse array: 1 (not zero), 0 (count_zeros=1), 1 (not zero), 0 (count_zeros=2), 1 (not zero). Final count_zeros=2. Step 2: Overwrite array. Length=5. For i=0: i<2, set arr[0]=0. i=1: i<2, set arr[1]=0. i=2: i>=2, set arr[2]=1. i=3: i>=2, set arr[3]=1. i=4: i>=2, set arr[4]=1. Result: [0, 0, 1, 1, 1].

Step 1: Count zeros. Traverse array: 0 (count_zeros=1), 0 (count_zeros=2), 0 (count_zeros=3), 1 (not zero), 1 (not zero). Final count_zeros=3. Step 2: Overwrite array. Length=5. For i=0: i<3, set arr[0]=0. i=1: i<3, set arr[1]=0. i=2: i<3, set arr[2]=0. i=3: i>=3, set arr[3]=1. i=4: i>=3, set arr[4]=1. Result: [0, 0, 0, 1, 1] (already sorted).

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Count Zeros in an Array
ladders.desc: Implement a function to count the number of zeros in an array containing only 0s and 1s.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the first pass of the two-pass counting approach. The intuition is straightforward: to sort the array, we need to know how many 0s are present so we can place them at the beginning. By iterating through each element, we check if it equals 0 and increment a counter accordingly. This works because the array contains only binary values, so counting zeros directly gives us the information needed for sorting. The algorithm is simple: initialize a counter to 0, loop through each element in the array, and if the element is 0, increment the counter. After the loop, the counter holds the total number of zeros. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a single integer variable. Connection to L0: This count is used in the full solution to determine how many positions to fill with 0s. Common mistakes include not initializing the counter, incorrectly comparing values (e.g., using '=' instead of '=='), or off-by-one errors in the loop. To avoid these, ensure proper variable initialization and use equality checks. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def count_zeros(arr): count = 0; for num in arr: if num == 0: count += 1; return count.

examples.trace: Initialize count=0. Element 1: not 0, count=0. Element 0: is 0, count=1. Element 1: not 0, count=1. Element 0: is 0, count=2. Element 1: not 0, count=2. Return 2.
Initialize count=0. Element 0: is 0, count=1. Element 0: is 0, count=2. Element 0: is 0, count=3. Element 1: not 0, count=3. Element 1: not 0, count=3. Return 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Overwrite Array Based on Count
ladders.desc: Given a count of zeros, overwrite an array to place zeros first followed by ones.
ladders.explanation: This core logic forms the second pass of the two-pass counting approach. The intuition is that once we know how many zeros are in the array (from the counting sub-routine), we can reconstruct the sorted array by filling the first 'count_zeros' positions with 0s and the remaining positions with 1s. This works because the sorted order for binary arrays is all 0s before all 1s, so the count directly tells us the split point. The algorithm involves iterating through the array indices: for each index i, if i is less than count_zeros, set the element to 0; otherwise, set it to 1. This ensures that exactly count_zeros zeros are placed at the start. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass through the array. Space complexity is O(1) as we modify the array in-place. Connection to L1: This step uses the count from the counting sub-routine to perform the overwriting. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., using <= instead of <), misplacing the condition for setting values, or not handling the array modification properly. To avoid these, double-check the loop range and condition logic. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def overwrite_array(arr, count_zeros): for i in range(len(arr)): if i < count_zeros: arr[i] = 0; else: arr[i] = 1; return arr.

examples.trace: Array length=5, count_zeros=2. i=0: 0<2, set arr[0]=0. i=1: 1<2, set arr[1]=0. i=2: 2>=2, set arr[2]=1. i=3: 3>=2, set arr[3]=1. i=4: 4>=2, set arr[4]=1. Result: [0, 0, 1, 1, 1].
Array length=5, count_zeros=3. i=0: 0<3, set arr[0]=0. i=1: 1<3, set arr[1]=0. i=2: 2<3, set arr[2]=0. i=3: 3>=3, set arr[3]=1. i=4: 4>=3, set arr[4]=1. Result: [0, 0, 0, 1, 1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Modify Array Elements
ladders.desc: Practice iterating through an array and modifying each element based on its index.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for the overwriting step in the two-pass counting approach. The intuition is that to sort the array, we need to access each position and set its value according to a rule (based on index compared to a count). This involves basic array manipulation: looping through indices, checking a condition, and assigning new values. The algorithm is to loop from 0 to length-1, and for each index i, perform an action (e.g., set to 0 or 1). This works because arrays allow direct index-based access and modification. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop. Space complexity is O(1) if modifying in-place. Connection to L2: This skill is used to implement the overwrite logic where indices determine whether to place 0 or 1. Common mistakes include incorrect loop ranges (off-by-one errors), not using proper index variables, or modifying the array incorrectly. To avoid these, ensure the loop runs for the correct number of iterations and use clear conditional statements. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def modify_array(arr): for i in range(len(arr)): # Perform modification based on i; e.g., arr[i] = some_value.

examples.trace: Array length=5. i=0: set arr[0]=0. i=1: set arr[1]=1. i=2: set arr[2]=2. i=3: set arr[3]=3. i=4: set arr[4]=4. Result: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4].
Array length=3. i=0: set arr[0]=0*2=0. i=1: set arr[1]=1*2=2. i=2: set arr[2]=2*2=4. Result: [0, 2, 4].

input.rule: set to 1 if index even, else 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Conditional Assignment in a Loop
ladders.desc: Learn to use if-else statements inside a loop to assign values based on a condition.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is a fundamental programming skill used in the two-pass counting approach. The intuition is that when processing array elements, we often need to make decisions (e.g., increment a counter if a value is 0, or set an element to 0 or 1 based on index). This involves combining loops with conditional logic. The algorithm is to iterate through a sequence and, for each element, check a condition using an if statement; if true, perform one action, else perform another. This works because it allows dynamic behavior based on data values. Time complexity depends on the loop length. Space complexity is typically O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used when modifying array elements based on index conditions. Common mistakes include incorrect condition syntax (e.g., missing parentheses), not covering all cases in if-else, or misplacing braces. To avoid these, practice writing clear conditions and test with different inputs. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: for item in collection: if condition: # do something; else: # do something else.

examples.trace: Iterate through list: 1 -> not 0, output 'one'. 0 -> is 0, output 'zero'. 1 -> not 0, output 'one'. 0 -> is 0, output 'zero'. 1 -> not 0, output 'one'. Result: ['one', 'zero', 'one', 'zero', 'one'].
Iterate through list: 5 -> <=10, output 'low'. 10 -> <=10, output 'low'. 15 -> >10, output 'high'. 20 -> >10, output 'high'. Result: ['low', 'low', 'high', 'high'].

input.condition: if value == 1, output 'a', elif value == 2, output 'b', else 'c'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Count Occurrences of a Value in a List
ladders.desc: Learn to count how many times a specific value appears in a list.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the two-pass counting approach. The intuition is basic: to sort an array of 0s and 1s, we first need to know how many 0s there are, which requires counting. Counting involves iterating through each element and incrementing a counter when the element matches the target value. This works because we can examine each item one by one and keep a running total. The algorithm is: initialize a counter to 0, loop through each element in the list, if the element equals the target value, add 1 to the counter. After the loop, the counter holds the count. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing the list. Space complexity is O(1) as only a single variable is used. Connection to L4: This uses conditional assignment in a loop to decide when to increment the counter. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize the counter, using the wrong comparison operator, or not looping through all elements. To avoid these, start with counter = 0 and ensure the loop covers the entire list. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def count_value(lst, target): count = 0; for item in lst: if item == target: count += 1; return count.

examples.trace: Initialize count=0. Item 1: not 0, count=0. Item 0: is 0, count=1. Item 1: not 0, count=1. Item 0: is 0, count=2. Item 1: not 0, count=2. Return 2.
Initialize count=0. Item apple: matches, count=1. Item banana: not apple, count=1. Item apple: matches, count=2. Item orange: not apple, count=2. Return 2.

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n) with In-Place Swap
approaches.summary: Use two pointers, one at the start and one at the end, to swap 1s from the left with 0s from the right until they meet. This sorts the array in a single pass without extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Array of 0s and 1s Using Two-Pointer In-Place Swap
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the two-pointer approach to sort an array containing only 0s and 1s in a single pass without extra space.
ladders.explanation:
The two-pointer in-place swap approach solves the sorting problem efficiently by maintaining two pointers: one starting at the leftmost index (left) and one at the rightmost index (right). The intuition is that we want all 0s on the left side and all 1s on the right side. We iterate while left <= right. At each step, if arr[left] is 1 and arr[right] is 0, we swap them because a 1 on the left should move to the right and a 0 on the right should move to the left. If arr[left] is 0, it's already in the correct position, so we increment left. If arr[right] is 1, it's already in the correct position, so we decrement right. This process continues until the pointers meet, ensuring the array is sorted. The algorithm runs in O(n) time since each element is visited at most once, and O(1) space as it operates in-place without additional data structures. This approach is optimal for this problem because it minimizes operations and avoids extra memory. Common mistakes include incorrect pointer updates leading to infinite loops or missing swaps; ensure conditions are checked carefully. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the swap subroutine into the full sorting logic.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize left = 0, right = n-1 where n is array length.
2. While left <= right:
   a. If arr[left] == 1 and arr[right] == 0:
      - Swap arr[left] and arr[right].
      - Increment left.
      - Decrement right.
   b. Else if arr[left] == 0:
      - Increment left.
   c. Else if arr[right] == 1:
      - Decrement right.
3. The array is now sorted.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def sort_zeros_ones(arr):
    left, right = 0, len(arr)-1
    while left <= right:
        if arr[left] == 1 and arr[right] == 0:
            arr[left], arr[right] = arr[right], arr[left]
            left += 1
            right -= 1
        elif arr[left] == 0:
            left += 1
        elif arr[right] == 1:
            right -= 1
    return arr

examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [1,0,1,0,1,0], left=0, right=5
Step 1: left=0 (arr[0]=1), right=5 (arr[5]=0) -> swap -> arr=[0,0,1,0,1,1], left=1, right=4
Step 2: left=1 (arr[1]=0) -> increment left -> left=2
Step 3: left=2 (arr[2]=1), right=4 (arr[4]=1) -> decrement right -> right=3
Step 4: left=2 (arr[2]=1), right=3 (arr[3]=0) -> swap -> arr=[0,0,0,1,1,1], left=3, right=2
Loop ends as left=3 > right=2
Sorted array: [0,0,0,1,1,1]

Initial: arr = [0,1,1,0,1], left=0, right=4
Step 1: left=0 (arr[0]=0) -> increment left -> left=1
Step 2: left=1 (arr[1]=1), right=4 (arr[4]=1) -> decrement right -> right=3
Step 3: left=1 (arr[1]=1), right=3 (arr[3]=0) -> swap -> arr=[0,0,1,1,1], left=2, right=2
Step 4: left=2 (arr[2]=1), right=2 (arr[2]=1) -> decrement right -> right=1
Loop ends as left=2 > right=1
Sorted array: [0,0,1,1,1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Swap Elements Based on Two-Pointer Conditions
ladders.desc: Implement the core swapping logic that moves 1s from the left to the right and 0s from the right to the left when pointers meet specific conditions.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the two-pointer approach, responsible for swapping elements when a 1 is found on the left side and a 0 on the right side. The intuition is that in a sorted array of 0s and 1s, all 0s should precede all 1s. By swapping a left 1 with a right 0, we move misplaced elements toward their correct sides efficiently. The algorithm uses two indices, left and right, and checks if arr[left] is 1 and arr[right] is 0; if so, it swaps them and updates both pointers. This ensures progress toward sorting without redundant operations. Time complexity is O(1) per swap, and space is O(1) as it operates in-place. This feeds into L0 by being the critical operation within the main loop. Common mistakes include swapping without checking both conditions or incorrect pointer updates; always verify that left < right to avoid out-of-bounds access. The connection to L2 is that this subroutine relies on the core logic of pointer movement.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: array arr, indices left and right.
2. If left < right and arr[left] == 1 and arr[right] == 0:
   a. Swap arr[left] and arr[right].
   b. Return updated left and right (typically incremented and decremented).
3. Else, handle non-swap cases separately (in L0).

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def swap_if_needed(arr, left, right):
    if left < right and arr[left] == 1 and arr[right] == 0:
        arr[left], arr[right] = arr[right], arr[left]
        return left + 1, right - 1
    return left, right

examples.trace:
Initial: arr=[1,0,1,0], left=0, right=3
Check: left=0 < right=3, arr[0]=1, arr[3]=0 -> conditions met
Swap arr[0] and arr[3] -> arr=[0,0,1,1]
Update left=0+1=1, right=3-1=2
Return left=1, right=2

Initial: arr=[0,1,0,1], left=1, right=2
Check: left=1 < right=2, arr[1]=1, arr[2]=0 -> conditions met
Swap arr[1] and arr[2] -> arr=[0,0,1,1]
Update left=1+1=2, right=2-1=1
Return left=2, right=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Movement for In-Place Partitioning
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to move two pointers inward, skipping correctly placed elements and stopping when they cross.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the pointer movement strategy that enables efficient in-place partitioning. The intuition is that we want to separate 0s and 1s by moving a left pointer from the start to find 1s (which should be on the right) and a right pointer from the end to find 0s (which should be on the left). When left points to a 0, it's already correct, so we move left forward. When right points to a 1, it's already correct, so we move right backward. This continues until left and right meet or cross, indicating all elements are in the correct partition. This core logic reduces unnecessary swaps and ensures linear time complexity. It connects to L1 by providing the conditions for when to swap. Time complexity is O(n) as each pointer moves at most n steps, and space is O(1). Common mistakes include moving pointers incorrectly in swap cases or not handling edge cases like empty arrays; always update pointers based on conditions. The connection to L3 is that this builds on the concept of array traversal.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize left = 0, right = n-1.
2. While left <= right:
   a. If arr[left] == 0: increment left.
   b. If arr[right] == 1: decrement right.
   c. Else (arr[left] == 1 and arr[right] == 0): proceed to swap (handled in L1).
3. Stop when left > right.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def move_pointers(arr, left, right):
    while left <= right:
        if arr[left] == 0:
            left += 1
        elif arr[right] == 1:
            right -= 1
        else:
            break  # Ready for swap in L1
    return left, right

examples.trace:
Initial: left=0, right=3
Step 1: arr[left]=1 (not 0), arr[right]=0 (not 1) -> break
Return left=0, right=3

Initial: left=0, right=3
Step 1: arr[left]=0 -> increment left -> left=1
Step 2: arr[left]=1 (not 0), arr[right]=1 -> decrement right -> right=2
Step 3: arr[left]=1 (not 0), arr[right]=0 (not 1) -> break
Return left=1, right=2

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse Array with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Implement a function to traverse an array from both ends simultaneously using two pointers until they meet.
ladders.explanation:
This building block introduces the technique of using two pointers to traverse an array from opposite ends, a fundamental pattern in many algorithms. The intuition is that by starting one pointer at the beginning and another at the end, we can process elements in a single pass, often reducing time complexity. Here, we focus on moving the pointers inward based on a simple condition (e.g., moving left forward if it's less than right) until they cross. This is a prerequisite for the sorting logic, as it sets up the framework for the two-pointer approach. Time complexity is O(n) since each pointer moves at most n/2 steps, and space is O(1). It connects to L2 by providing the traversal mechanism needed for partitioning. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in pointer updates or infinite loops; ensure the loop condition (left <= right) is correct. The connection to L4 is that this builds on basic array indexing.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize left = 0, right = n-1.
2. While left <= right:
   a. Process elements at arr[left] and arr[right] (e.g., print or compare).
   b. Increment left.
   c. Decrement right.
3. Stop when left > right.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def traverse_two_pointers(arr):
    left, right = 0, len(arr)-1
    while left <= right:
        # Example: print elements
        print(arr[left], arr[right])
        left += 1
        right -= 1

examples.trace:
Initial: left=0, right=3
Step 1: left=0, right=3 -> print arr[0]=1 and arr[3]=4
Update: left=1, right=2
Step 2: left=1, right=2 -> print arr[1]=2 and arr[2]=3
Update: left=2, right=1
Loop ends as left=2 > right=1

Initial: left=0, right=2
Step 1: left=0, right=2 -> print arr[0]=5 and arr[2]=7
Update: left=1, right=1
Step 2: left=1, right=1 -> print arr[1]=6 and arr[1]=6
Update: left=2, right=0
Loop ends as left=2 > right=0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swap Two Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Implement a function to swap the values at two given indices in an array.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation is fundamental to many algorithms, including the two-pointer sort. The intuition is that swapping exchanges the values at two positions, allowing rearrangement of elements without extra space. In the context of sorting 0s and 1s, swapping moves misplaced 0s and 1s to their correct sides. The algorithm is straightforward: store the value at index i in a temporary variable, assign the value at index j to index i, then assign the temporary value to index j. This ensures the swap is done correctly without losing data. Time complexity is O(1), and space is O(1) for the temporary variable. It connects to L3 as swapping is used during traversal in the two-pointer approach. Common mistakes include forgetting the temporary variable or swapping indices instead of values; always use a temp variable to avoid overwriting. The connection to L5 is that this relies on array indexing concepts.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: array arr, indices i and j.
2. If i and j are valid indices within array bounds:
   a. temp = arr[i]
   b. arr[i] = arr[j]
   c. arr[j] = temp
3. Output: updated arr.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def swap_elements(arr, i, j):
    if 0 <= i < len(arr) and 0 <= j < len(arr):
        arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]
    return arr

examples.trace:
Initial: arr=[1,0,1], i=0, j=2
Check indices: 0 and 2 are valid
Swap: temp = arr[0]=1
arr[0] = arr[2]=1 -> arr=[1,0,1]
arr[2] = temp=1 -> arr=[1,0,1]
Return [1,0,1]

Initial: arr=[5,10,15], i=1, j=2
Check indices: 1 and 2 are valid
Swap: temp = arr[1]=10
arr[1] = arr[2]=15 -> arr=[5,15,15]
arr[2] = temp=10 -> arr=[5,15,10]
Return [5,15,10]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access and Modify Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Understand how to read and write values at specific positions in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept is essential for all array manipulations, including the two-pointer sort. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices starting from 0. The intuition is that each element has a unique position (index), allowing direct access in O(1) time. To read an element, use arr[i]; to modify it, assign a new value like arr[i] = value. This is the basis for operations like swapping and traversal. In the context of sorting 0s and 1s, we use indices to check if an element is 0 or 1 and to move elements by reassigning values. Time complexity for access/modify is O(1), and space is O(1) per operation. It connects to L4 as swapping relies on indexing to exchange values. Common mistakes include using out-of-bounds indices or confusing indices with values; always ensure indices are within 0 to len(arr)-1. This concept is beginner-friendly and prerequisite for all higher-level array algorithms.

Step-by-step explanation:
1. Array declaration: e.g., arr = [1, 0, 1].
2. Access element at index i: value = arr[i] (e.g., arr[0] gives 1).
3. Modify element at index i: arr[i] = new_value (e.g., arr[0] = 0 changes array to [0,0,1]).
4. Always check index bounds to avoid errors.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
# Example: read and modify
arr = [1, 0, 1]
first_element = arr[0]  # Reads 1
arr[1] = 1  # Modifies array to [1, 1, 1]

examples.input: arr=[5,10,15], read index=1, modify index=2 to 20
examples.trace:
Initial array: [5,10,15]
Read arr[1]: value = 10
Modify arr[2] = 20: array becomes [5,10,20]
Output read value 10 and modified array [5,10,20]

examples.input: arr=[0,1,0,1], read index=0, modify index=3 to 0
examples.trace:
Initial array: [0,1,0,1]
Read arr[0]: value = 0
Modify arr[3] = 0: array becomes [0,1,0,0]
Output read value 0 and modified array [0,1,0,0]

arr=[0,1,0], read index=2, modify index=0 to 1
